common_name: Peruvian Purple Pepper (Ornamental Edible Hot Pepper with Purple Foliage and Upright Fruits)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Peruvian Purple is a compact ornamental edible pepper (18–24 inches) with distinctive deep purple-black foliage, purple stems, and small upright fruits (1.5–2 inches) that mature from purple-black through a transitional stage to vivid red. The purple pigmentation in both foliage and immature fruits results from anthocyanin accumulation triggered by light exposure, making it one of the most visually striking ornamental peppers available. Heat level is high (estimated 50,000–80,000 SHU), reflecting the small fruit size typical of high-heat Capsicum annuum types. In zone 9, transplant March–April; the compact habit makes it excellent for containers and ornamental mixed plantings where edible and decorative plants are combined.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost to full sun and harvest fruits at any stage from purple-black (immature, high heat) to fully red (ripe). Peruvian Purple Pepper is a compact ornamental-edible hot pepper variety distinguished by its unusual anthocyanin-rich foliage — a deep purple-black that makes the plant visually dramatic from seedling stage through maturity. The purple coloration in the leaves and immature fruits results from anthocyanin pigments produced in response to high light intensity; the same plant grown in reduced light will show less purple coloration and more green foliage, making sun exposure a direct visible factor in the plant's ornamental effect. Fruits emerge deep purple-black and upright — pointing skyward rather than pendant — which is a characteristic of many small ornamental hot pepper types and adds visual interest as the small fruits are held prominently above the purple foliage. As fruits ripen to full maturity they transition through a mottled purple-and-red stage before reaching solid vivid red; many gardeners harvest throughout this color progression since the flavor and heat level remain high at all stages. The heat level is substantial for a Capsicum annuum variety — comparable to Thai or bird's eye types — and is proportional to the small fruit size, as capsaicin concentration tends to increase as fruit diameter decreases within the species. Culinary uses mirror those of other small hot peppers: used fresh in salads, salsas, and stir-fries; pickled; or dried whole for later use. As a compact container plant with dramatic foliage, Peruvian Purple also functions effectively as a purely ornamental annual in mixed containers where its purple leaves provide contrast against chartreuse, silver, or deep green companions.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Peruvian Purple Pepper variety description and ornamental characteristics
Seed Savers Exchange — Purple-leaved Capsicum annuum varieties and anthocyanin expression notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Edible Peppers for Florida Home Gardens

common_name: New Mexico No. 6-4 Green Chile (Classic Hatch-Style Mild Roasting Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; New Mexico No. 6-4 is the foundational Hatch-type green chile developed at New Mexico State University in the 1950s from the original New Mexico No. 6. It produces long, pendant pods (6–8 inches) with mild-to-medium heat (1,000–4,000 SHU) that are the standard for roasting green chiles. In zone 9, transplant March–April; the Hatch valley grows these chiles from June through September in a desert climate, but the variety also performs in humid Gulf Coast conditions if given well-drained soil and good airflow to minimize fungal issues. Harvest green (before color break) for roasting; allow to ripen red for dried red chile powder.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost and stake lightly. Harvest green pods at full size before any color break for roasting, or allow to ripen to deep red for drying. New Mexico No. 6-4 is the chile pepper variety that defined an entire regional food culture: the mild, thin-walled roasting pepper grown in the Hatch Valley of southern New Mexico and marketed under the 'Hatch Chile' designation that drives an enormous annual harvest festival and roasting tradition every August and September in New Mexico and throughout the American Southwest. The variety was developed by Dr. Roy Harper at New Mexico State University in Albuquerque in the 1950s through selection from the original New Mexico No. 6 variety, with the '6-4' designation referring to the parents used in the cross. The resulting variety became the commercial standard for New Mexico green chile production because it combined the mild-to-medium heat level preferred by most consumers with a pod shape and size (6–8 inches long, 1.5–2 inches wide) that roasts evenly on a flame or in a large drum roaster without burning before the flesh is fully cooked through. Thin walls and high moisture content allow steam to separate the charred skin from the flesh during roasting, enabling the characteristic step of peeling the blackened skin to reveal the moist, mildly pungent chile beneath. Home gardeners can replicate commercial roasting by placing whole pods under a broiler or directly on a gas burner flame, turning until charred on all sides, then placing in a sealed plastic bag for 10–15 minutes to steam before peeling. For red chile production, allow all pods to fully ripen to deep red on the plant or allow to ripen after harvest in a warm, dry location before drying whole or grinding to powder.
sources: New Mexico State University Chile Pepper Institute — NM 6-4 variety origin, development history, and production standards
Paul W. Bosland and David Votava, 'Peppers: Vegetable and Spice Capsicums' (CABI, 2012) — Hatch variety documentation
NMSU Extension — Home Production of New Mexico Chile Peppers

common_name: Musica Romano Pole Bean (Wide Flat Italian-Style Pole Bean with Exceptional Tenderness)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 60°F; Musica is a Dutch-bred flat Romano-type pole bean with unusually wide, flat pods (up to 1 inch wide) reaching 8–10 inches long. It requires a trellis or pole support to 6–7 feet. Unlike round-podded pole beans that become stringy and coarse as they size up, Musica maintains remarkable tenderness and sweet flavor well past the size at which most beans become tough — pods can be harvested at full size (9–10 inches) without significant loss of texture. This characteristic makes Musica one of the most forgiving pole beans for busy gardeners who cannot harvest daily. In zone 9, direct sow March–April after last frost for spring production and again August–September for fall harvest.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart; provide a sturdy trellis or poles to 7 feet and harvest pods when flat and full-length (8–10 inches) but before seeds develop prominently inside. Musica Romano Pole Bean is a Dutch commercial variety developed specifically to combine the flat Romano-style pod shape beloved in Italian cuisine with the productivity and extended harvest window of a modern pole bean. The flat pod shape — wide (nearly 1 inch across) with a slightly rectangular cross-section rather than round — is the defining characteristic of the Romano bean type, which originated in Italy and represents a fundamentally different pod morphology from the round, pencil-thin beans of French filet types or the oval cross-section of standard American snap beans. Romano types are prized for a distinctive beany, almost meaty flavor that is more pronounced than round-podded types; this flavor intensity comes partly from the thicker pod walls and slightly higher dry matter content of the flat pod structure. Musica improves on older Romano varieties in two key ways: pods remain tender and stringless at large sizes (a significant practical advantage, since older Romano types become stringy once pods exceed 6–7 inches), and the plant produces heavily over an extended harvest window rather than the concentrated burst typical of bush bean types. The vigorous climbing plant (to 7 feet) is highly productive in warm conditions and particularly well-suited to zone 9's long warm season, where it can continue producing through fall when given a midsummer break from heat. Steam or blanch briefly for the best texture; Musica also holds up well in longer-cooked dishes like minestrone where flat Romano beans contribute body without dissolving.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Musica Romano Pole Bean variety notes and harvest timing recommendations
Territorial Seed Company — Flat Romano pole bean comparison and Italian cooking applications
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Romano-type bean variety descriptions and culinary uses

common_name: Emerite Filet Bean (French Haricot Vert Pole Bean for Thin Tender Pods)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to 60°F; Emerite is a French pole-type filet bean (haricot vert) producing slender round pods (under 0.25 inch diameter) at pencil-thin harvest stage of 4–6 inches. The pole habit (to 5–6 feet) requires trellis support but produces far more heavily and over a much longer period than bush filet types. Harvest daily or every other day at pencil-thin stage for the finest texture; pods become starchy and coarse within 2–3 days of reaching optimal harvest size. Emerite is one of the most productive filet pole beans available, making it a practical choice for gardeners who want restaurant-quality haricots verts without the heavy labor input of bush varieties that must be replanted every 2–3 weeks.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart; provide trellis or pole support to 6 feet and harvest every 1–2 days when pods reach 4–5 inches and are no thicker than a pencil. Emerite Filet Bean is a French-bred pole-type haricot vert that delivers the classic thin-podded, tender filet bean quality of French cuisine on a vigorous climbing plant that produces heavily over months rather than the 2–3-week harvest window of bush filet types. The haricot vert category — defined by pod diameter under 6mm and harvest at pencil-thin stage before any internal seed development is visible — requires frequent harvesting to maintain pod quality; pods left on the plant for even an extra day begin to develop seeds and shift from the tender, fine-textured filet bean to a coarser snap bean. This harvesting discipline rewards with pods of extraordinary tenderness: lightly steamed for 3–4 minutes, Emerite haricots verts have a delicate, slightly squeaky texture and refined mild flavor that is categorically different from mature-harvested snap beans. Pole-type filet beans like Emerite provide a practical advantage over bush types for home production: a single planting in spring can continue producing through fall in zone 9 (with a brief mid-summer slowdown during peak heat), whereas bush filet varieties exhaust themselves within 2–3 weeks and require successive plantings every 3 weeks to maintain a continuous harvest. The climbing habit also improves air circulation around pods in the humid Gulf Coast environment, reducing the fungal disease pressure that can limit bush bean production in midsummer heat and humidity. Blanch harvested pods in salted boiling water for 2–3 minutes, shock in ice water to set the bright green color, then serve simply with butter, lemon, and salt to showcase the delicate flavor.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Emerite Filet Pole Bean variety notes and harvest timing for haricot vert production
Fedco Seeds — French pole filet bean varieties for extended season production
Vilmorin-Andrieux, 'The Vegetable Garden' (English translation) — French haricot vert bean culture and variety selection

common_name: Gold Rush Wax Bean (Bright Yellow Stringless Bush Wax Bean with Straight Pods)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil has warmed to at least 60°F; Gold Rush is a compact stringless yellow wax bush bean producing uniform straight pods (5–6 inches) in a concentrated set over 2–3 weeks. The bright golden-yellow color of the pods makes harvesting easy — the pods stand out clearly against green foliage, reducing the chance of overlooking mature pods that would become tough and seedy if missed. Successive sowings every 3 weeks through midsummer extend the harvest; in zone 9, sow February–April for spring and again September–October for fall production. Gold Rush is one of the highest-yielding yellow wax bush beans, with pods borne in dense clusters just above the soil surface on a compact upright plant.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep, 4–6 inches apart in rows 18–24 inches apart; harvest when pods reach 5–6 inches before seeds swell noticeably inside. Gold Rush Wax Bean is a high-yielding yellow wax bush bean developed by Burpee for home garden production, distinguished by the uniformity and straightness of its bright golden pods and its concentrated harvest set that makes it one of the most productive yellow wax types per planting. The wax bean category — yellow-podded varieties that lack the green chlorophyll of standard snap beans — developed historically as a distinct preference in European-American cooking traditions where the pale color was associated with tenderness and delicacy; wax beans are botanically identical to green snap beans, with the yellow coloration resulting from the absence of chlorophyll development in the pod walls, allowing carotenoid pigments (which would normally be masked by green) to express visibly. Gold Rush's pods are straight and uniform (an advantage for commercial packing but also convenient for home preparation), stringless from the earliest edible stage through full harvest size, and have a mild flavor with a slightly more buttery taste than equivalent green snap varieties — a characteristic that many cooks attribute to the different pigment chemistry of wax pods, though the underlying flavor compounds are essentially the same. The compact bush plant reaches 18–24 inches with an erect, self-supporting habit that keeps pods above soil contact and makes one-pass harvesting efficient. For highest yield and quality, harvest every 2–3 days throughout the peak bearing period and do not allow pods to yellow beyond the characteristic bright gold; overripe pods become pale, stringy, and starchy.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Gold Rush Wax Bean variety development notes and productivity data
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Yellow Wax Bush Bean Variety Comparison for Home Garden Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Snap Bean and Wax Bean Variety Performance in Florida
